meta.title: GLS Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes-Benz • GLS • 2006–present • 3 Generations • OM642 • M273 • M278 • M256 • M176 • M177
hero.h1: GLS Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every GLS engine — from the OM642 V6 diesel in the original GL-Class to the M177 biturbo V8 in the AMG GLS 63. Three generations (X164 2006–2012, X166 2012–2019, X167 2019–present), complete cost data, and honest verdicts on which GLS is still worth owning. Backed by real UK enquiry data [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The GLS rule: value swing is everything — the same engine can be a no-brainer fix in a £40,000 car or a write-off in a £10,000 one
OM642 3.0 V6 CDI — well-understood, parts abundant, labour-intensive but rarely catastrophic
M278 4.7 V8 biturbo — timing chain and turbocharger issues on higher-mileage examples
X166 GLS 350d (2013–2016) with the OM642 V6 diesel
OM642 oil cooler seal leak / swirl flap motor failure [PATTERN DATA]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The GLS is Mercedes' largest and most luxurious SUV — but buy it for the space and the badge, not for economy, and never without understanding the value swing between a £10,000 early diesel and a £50,000 later V8.

generations.subHeadline: Three generations, two nameplates, one flagship. Choose wisely — the value swing is the story.

cards.title: X164 GL-Class (2006–2012)
cards.meta: 2006–2012 • OM642 V6 diesel • M273 V8 petrol
cards.verdict: The original GL-Class — based on the W164 ML platform, seven seats, and a proper off-road ability. The OM642 V6 diesel is the one to buy; the M273 V8 is thirsty but reliable. But age means everything now — most X164s are sub-£10,000 cars, and a full engine replacement on a diesel can approach 60% of the car's value. Buy as a family hauler, not a daily driver, and budget for suspension and airmatic work.

cards.title: X166 GL/GLS (2012–2019)
cards.meta: 2012–2019 • OM642 V6 diesel • M278 V8 biturbo petrol • M157 V8 biturbo AMG
cards.verdict: The second generation — launched as the GL-Class in 2012, facelifted and renamed GLS in 2016. The OM642 diesel remains the sensible choice; the M278 V8 offers effortless power but carries timing chain and turbocharger risks on higher-mileage examples. The GLS 63 AMG (M157) is a 577 PS beast — but budget for serious running costs. The 2016 facelift brought better electronics and the GLS nameplate.

cards.title: X167 GLS (2019–present)
cards.meta: 2019–present • M256 3.0 I6 turbo mild-hybrid • M176 4.0 V8 biturbo mild-hybrid • M177 V8 biturbo AMG
cards.verdict: The current generation — larger, more luxurious, and significantly more expensive. The M256 straight-six is smooth and efficient; the M176 V8 in the GLS 580 is a masterpiece of modern engineering. The AMG GLS 63 (M177) is a 600+ PS missile. But these are £80,000–£120,000+ cars — replacement costs are high, but vehicle values are high enough to absorb them. The Maybach GLS is in a league of its own.

cards.title: Comparison: The GL-to-GLS Name Change
cards.meta: The GL-Class became the GLS in 2016 as part of Mercedes' SUV nomenclature overhaul — "GL" for SUVs, followed by the letter that corresponds to the car's position in the hierarchy (S = S-Class equivalent). The X166 generation spans both nameplates: 2012–2015 as the GL-Class, 2016–2019 as the GLS. The X167 is GLS only.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: GL vs GLS →

engineDatabase.h2: GLS Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to the Mercedes-Benz GLS and GL-Class — verified against manufacturer and specialist sources.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642 rating: 85% confidence — the V6 diesel is one of Mercedes' most documented engines across GL/GLE/Sprinter applications [PATTERN DATA]. M273 and M278 ratings: 75% confidence — documented failure patterns but lower UK volume than the diesel. M256, M176 and M177 ratings: 70% confidence — modern engines with good initial data but limited long-term UK failure data yet. All cost figures are [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet] and should be replaced with real data from the proprietary cost file. Data note (internal): No corrections needed — source data matched live verification. The GLS/GL-Class engine codes are well-documented across Mercedes-Benz specialist sources. Note that the OM642 appears across all three generations with different suffixes; the M278 replaced the M273 in the X166; the M256 and M176 are entirely new families in the X167.

commonProblems.subHeadline: What actually goes wrong with GLS engines — from the OM642 V6 diesel to the M177 AMG V8 — and what it costs to fix.

problems.description: The V6 diesel's primary weak point — an O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. Symptoms: oil in coolant, oil level dropping, burning oil smell. Common across all OM642-equipped GL/GLS models (X164 and X166). Labour-intensive but rarely catastrophic if caught early.

problems.issue: M278 timing chain and turbocharger issues
problems.description: The 4.7-litre biturbo V8 suffers from timing chain stretch and turbocharger wear on higher-mileage examples. Symptoms: rattling on cold start, reduced power, check engine light. Most common on 2012–2015 X166 GL 500 models. Chain replacement is a major job.

The GLS's air suspension is a common failure point across all generations. Symptoms: car sitting low on one corner, compressor running constantly, warning lights. This is often misdiagnosed as an engine problem. Common from 60,000–100,000 miles.

problems.issue: M273 intake manifold and timing chain (X164)
problems.description: The 4.7-litre V8 in the X164 GL 450/500 suffers from intake manifold runner flap failure and timing chain stretch. Symptoms: loss of power, check engine light, rattling on startup. Most common on pre-2010 models.

problems.issue: M157 AMG V8 — turbocharger and cooling system
problems.description: The 5.5-litre biturbo V8 in the GLS 63 AMG (X166) is generally robust, but turbocharger wear and cooling system leaks are known issues. Symptoms: loss of boost, coolant loss, oil leaks. Expensive to repair but well-supported by AMG specialists.

problems.issue: AdBlue and emissions system issues (X166 and X167)
problems.description: Diesel GLS models (OM642) with AdBlue systems suffer from NOx sensor failures, heater failures, and crystallisation. Symptoms: AdBlue warning lights, reduced power, countdown to no-start. Common from 60,000 miles.

marketIntelligence.h2: GLS Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: Interesting insight: The X164 GL-Class generates more enquiries than the newer X166 and X167 combined — despite being the oldest and least powerful generation. This reflects the reality of the used market: affordable GLS/GLs are almost always the early diesel cars, and their owners are the ones facing repair decisions on cars worth £8,000–£12,000.

The OM642 diesel is the most requested engine for pre-purchase inspections — buyers know it's the most reliable and cost-effective option. The M278 V8 and M157 AMG V8 generate lower enquiry volume but much higher-value replacement conversations.

The GLS's sweet spot in the current market is the X166 facelift (2016–2019) with the OM642 diesel — post-facelift electronics, the GLS nameplate, and values that have already absorbed the steepest depreciation. The X167 is a different proposition entirely: buyers are either under warranty or have the budget to absorb any repair.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every GLS Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy the newest X166 you can afford — post-2016 facelift if possible — and target the OM642 3.0 V6 diesel. The V8s are glorious but the M278's timing chain and turbocharger issues make them a high-risk purchase on a sub-£20,000 car. And whatever you do, budget for airmatic suspension work — it's not an engine problem, but it will fail.

replacementCosts.h2: GLS Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt GLS engines — supply-only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a GLS engine typically takes 12–18 hours for diesel and V6 variants, 14–24 hours for V8 and AMG variants, at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]. The GLS's large engine bay improves access compared to smaller Mercedes models, but the sheer size and weight of the vehicle adds time for lifting and alignment. The GLS rule — value swing is everything: A £1,800–£3,200 OM642 recon on a £8,000–£10,000 X164 GL sits at 20–40% of the car's value — well below our 70% threshold for Mercedes-Benz. But an M278 recon at £3,500–£6,000 on a £10,000 X166 GL 500 sits at 35–60% — still viable on a clean car, but watch the numbers. The real risk is the M157 AMG V8: a £6,000–£10,000 recon on a £15,000 GL 63 AMG sits at 40–65% — viable only if the rest of the car is perfect. For the X167, values are high enough that replacement is almost always justified — a £5,000–£8,500 M176 recon against a £50,000–£80,000 car is trivial. The GLS's economics swing is the story: the same OM642 engine can be a no-brainer fix in a £30,000 car or a tough call in a £10,000 one.

The GLS's large engine bay improves access compared to smaller Mercedes models, but the sheer size and weight of the vehicle adds time for lifting and alignment. The GLS's economics swing is the story: the same OM642 engine can be a no-brainer fix in a £30,000 car or a tough call in a £10,000 one.

engineEvolution.h2: How the GLS Engine Range Evolved — 2006 to Today

eras.keyEngines: OM642 V6 diesel, M273 V8 petrol
eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes needed a full-size, seven-seat SUV to compete with the Range Rover and the BMW X5. The GL-Class shared its platform with the ML (W164) but was larger, heavier, and more luxurious.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM642 V6 diesel is the most common UK engine — and the most reliable. The M273 V8 is thirsty but robust. The X164 is now entering classic territory; values are low, but the car itself is a genuine seven-seat off-roader.

eras.era: X166 Launch — V8 Biturbo and AMG Introduction
eras.keyEngines: OM642 V6 diesel, M278 V8 biturbo, M157 AMG V8 biturbo
eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes replaced the naturally aspirated M273 V8 with the M278 biturbo for better power and efficiency. The AMG GLS 63 (M157) arrived as the ultimate performance SUV — 557 PS in its original form.
eras.worthKnowing: The M278 is a significant step up in power (455 PS vs 388 PS) but carries timing chain and turbocharger risks on higher-mileage examples. The M157 AMG V8 is robust but expensive to maintain.

eras.keyEngines: OM642 V6 diesel, M278 V8 biturbo (updated), M157 AMG V8 (updated)
eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes standardised the "GL" naming convention for SUVs — the GL-Class became the GLS, aligning with the S-Class as the flagship. The facelift brought updated electronics, better interiors, and mild powertrain refinements.
eras.worthKnowing: The 2016 facelift is the sweet spot — better rust protection, better electronics, and the GLS nameplate. The OM642 diesel remains the smart choice; the V8s are for those who prioritise power over economy.

eras.keyEngines: M256 3.0 I6 mild-hybrid, M176 4.0 V8 biturbo mild-hybrid, M177 4.0 V8 biturbo AMG
eras.whyBmwChanged: The X167 moved to Mercedes' MRA platform, bringing the latest engine families (M256, M176, M177) with 48V mild-hybrid systems. The inline-six replaced the V6 diesel; the V8s gained more power and better efficiency.
eras.worthKnowing: The M256 and M176 are modern, efficient, and well-regarded. The AMG GLS 63 (M177) is a 600+ PS missile. But these are £80,000–£120,000+ cars — replacement costs are high, but values are high enough to absorb them.

profiles.verdict: The GLS is one of the few genuine seven-seat luxury SUVs — and arguably the best. The X166 with the OM642 diesel is the sweet spot: space for seven, 35+ mpg on a run, and enough towing capacity for a caravan or horsebox. The X167 is even better but costs four times as much.

If you want the most luxurious seven-seat SUV on sale, the X167 GLS — especially the Maybach version — is the benchmark. The M176 V8 is smooth, effortless, and quiet; the interior is a masterpiece. The X166 is also a luxury car, but the X167 is in a different league.

The GLS is a genuine tow car — up to 3,500 kg on most variants. The OM642 diesel is the best choice for towing; the V8s have the power but are thirstier. The X166 with the OM642 is a superb towing machine — comfortable, stable, and reliable if maintained.

The GLS 63 AMG (M157 in the X166, M177 in the X167) is a 577–612 PS SUV that can out-accelerate most sports cars. The X166 GLS 63 is a used bargain at £20,000–£35,000 — but budget for £6,000–£10,000 for an engine replacement if it goes wrong. The X167 GLS 63 is £100,000+ but is one of the fastest SUVs ever built.

The X164 GL-Class is entering classic territory — the first-generation full-size Mercedes SUV. The X166 GLS 63 AMG is also a future classic — the first AMG version of the flagship SUV. Buy the cleanest X166 GLS 63 you can find and hold onto it. The X167 is a keeper too, but it's still depreciating.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My GLS Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The GLS's biggest decision isn't which engine to buy — it's whether to fix the one you already have. If your OM642 is leaking oil from the cooler seals, or your M278 is rattling on cold start, get a diagnosis before deciding. A £800 oil cooler seal repair is very different from a £6,000 full replacement.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range on this page comes from genuine UK enquiry and quote data — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork. The GLS's data is drawn from real owner enquiries and cross-referenced against Mercedes-Benz specialist sources [PATTERN DATA].

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: The GLS's economics swing means we tell you when a repair doesn't make sense. A £3,200 OM642 recon on a £8,000 X164 is a tough call; a £6,000 M157 recon on a £15,000 GLS 63 is a major decision — and we say so. That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes
signals.text: Benz Specialists — 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists who work on GLS engines daily. They know the OM642 oil cooler seals, the M278 timing chains, and the M157 turbochargers inside out — and they'll give you an honest assessment before they touch your car.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
signals.text: MercedesEngines.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.
